plan_row.tema: The Hard Conversation -- Giving Difficult Feedback

heading: Now You Are the
Difficult One
warmup.intro: Last class you disagreed with your stakeholder, and you closed like this:

callback.text: I do not agree, but I will support the decision.
callback.speak: I do not agree, but I will support the decision.

warmup.box_title: Today the power is on your side, and that makes it harder.
warmup.box_text: Marina is a strong developer. She has missed the last three sprint deadlines, and she says nothing about it. Nobody has told her. Today you do.

goal.d: Three role-plays, and she gets defensive

heading: The Language of Hard
Feedback
vocab_ch.sub: 8 words to say the difficult thing, and keep the person

vocab.def: What you tell someone about their work, so it improves
vocab.def_pc: what you tell someone about their work, so it improves
vocab.hint: What you tell someone about their work
vocab.ex: Can I give you some feedback?

vocab.def: What a person does -- not who they are
vocab.def_pc: what a person does, not who they are
vocab.hint: What a person does, not who they are
vocab.ex: I am talking about the behavior, not about you.

vocab.def: Exact: a date, a number, one event
vocab.def_pc: exact: a date, a number, one event
vocab.hint: Exact: a date, a number, one event
vocab.ex: Be specific: three sprints, three delays.

vocab.def: Ready to protect yourself, instead of listening
vocab.def_pc: ready to protect yourself instead of listening
vocab.ex: She gets defensive when I mention the sprint.
Honest is good. Harsh is not.
I am not sure she is aware of the impact.

vocab.def: To change something a little, to make it work
vocab.def_pc: to change something a little, to make it work
vocab.hint: To change a little, so it works
vocab.ex: We can adjust the plan together.

vocab.def: To say clearly that someone did well
vocab.def_pc: to say clearly that someone did well
vocab.hint: To say clearly that someone did well
vocab.ex: Praise in public, correct in private.

heading: Feedback You Never
Gave
vocab_check.prompt: Think of someone at Oracle you should have talked to, and never did.
vocab_check.sub: Talk for 90 seconds: what did they do, what did it cost the project, and why did you stay silent?

reading.ch_sub: A short text about why most feedback fails
heading: Feedback Is Not a
Speech
paras: Most managers give feedback badly, and they give it late. They wait for the review, they write a long speech, and then they say something vague: 'You need to be more proactive.' The person hears nothing useful, and nothing changes.

Good feedback is short, specific and about behavior. Not 'you are careless', but 'the last three sprints closed two days late, and the test team lost two days each time'. One is an attack on a person. The other is a fact, and a fact can be discussed.

Then comes the part most people forget: the question. 'What is getting in the way?' Ask it, and be quiet. The answer is usually the real problem -- and it is often something you can fix. Feedback is not a speech. It is a conversation, and the other person has to speak.

choices: Feedback should be saved for the yearly review, when you have more examples.
Good feedback is short, specific, about behavior -- and it ends with a question.
The manager should do most of the talking, to be clear.

tf: 'You need to be more proactive' is specific feedback.
It is vague. Specific means a date, a number, one event.
Feedback should describe behavior, not the person.
'The last three sprints closed late' -- a fact, not an attack.
After the feedback, you should ask a question and listen.
'What is getting in the way?' -- then be quiet.

heading: How to Say the Hard
Thing
bank.sub: The behavior → the impact → the question → the ask → the support
bank.label: The five lines of hard feedback
items: Can I give you some feedback? It is not easy, but it matters.
The last three sprints closed two days late.
The impact is that the test team loses two days, every sprint.
What is getting in the way?
I need you to flag the risk on day three, not on the last day.
You are strong technically, and I want this to be the only thing we fix.

grammar.prompt: Which one attacks? Which one describes? Which one opens a conversation?

discovery.html: "When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team."
discovery.speak: When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team.

discovery.html: "The last three sprints closed two days late."
discovery.speak: The last three sprints closed two days late.

discovery.html: "What is getting in the way?"
discovery.speak: What is getting in the way?

discovery.html: "I need you to flag the risk on day three."
discovery.speak: I need you to flag the risk on day three.

rows: Past simple + a number (never an adjective about the person)
The last three sprints closed two days late.
When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days.
What is getting in the way? + I need you to + verb
What is getting in the way? ... I need you to flag it earlier.
grammar.note: Attack the behavior, never the person. "You are careless" is an opinion, and the only possible answer is defence. "Three sprints closed two days late" is a fact, and a fact can be discussed, explained -- and fixed.
mistakes: You are always late. You are not committed.
The last three sprints closed two days late.
I want you send the risk on day three.
I want you to send the risk on day three.
grammar.mistake_note: Facts, not labels. And after want / need / ask, English uses you TO + verb -- never "I want you send".

heading: The Feedback
Note
artefact.title: One Behavior, One Ask
artefact.reason: Behavior: the last three sprints closed two days late
rows: Impact: the test team loses two days in every sprint
Question: what is getting in the way?
Ask: flag the risk on day three, not on the last day
Praise (true, not a sandwich): the migration script was the best work on the team
artefact.footer: One behavior. One ask. Fifteen minutes, and she does most of the talking.

heading: Sophia Will Not Do It for
You
listenings.text: Diogo, I read your sprint report. Three sprints late, and the reason is always the same person. You have not spoken to her, have you? I know it is uncomfortable. But if you do not say it, two things happen. She never gets better, because nobody told her. And the team sees that late is acceptable. Do not be harsh, and do not make a speech. Be specific: what happened, how many days, what it costs the test team. Then ask her one question, and be quiet. Ninety percent of the time the answer surprises you. And Diogo, do not send an email. Say it to her face.
comp: What has Diogo not done yet?
He has not spoken to Marina about the late sprints.
What two things happen if he stays silent?
She never improves, and the team learns that late is acceptable.
What does Sophia tell him to do after the feedback?
Ask one question, and then be quiet.

heading: The Feedback,
Done Well
listenings.lead: This is the target. Listen, then do it your way.
listenings.text: Marina, thank you for the time. Can I give you some feedback? It is not easy, and I want to be specific. The last three sprints closed two days late. I am talking about the deadline, not about the quality of your code -- the migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is real: the test team loses two days, every time, and they work the weekend. So my question is simple. What is getting in the way? Please be honest with me. I am not here to blame you. I am here because I need you to flag the risk on day three, not on the last day, so we can adjust the plan together. If something is blocking you, tell me and I will remove it.
comp: The last three sprints closed two days late.
The test team loses two days every sprint, and works the weekend.
What does he ask her to do in the future?
Flag the risk on day three, not on the last day.

heading: She Gets
Defensive

lines.html: Marina, can I give you some feedback? The last three sprints closed two days late.
lines.speak: Marina, can I give you some feedback? The last three sprints closed two days late.

lines.html: The tickets came late. That is not my fault.
lines.speak: The tickets came late. That is not my fault.

lines.html: I am not talking about fault. I am talking about the behavior, and I want to be specific: sprint twelve, thirteen and fourteen.
lines.speak: I am not talking about fault. I am talking about the behavior, and I want to be specific: sprint twelve, thirteen and fourteen.

lines.html: So now I am the problem of the project?
lines.speak: So now I am the problem of the project?

lines.html: No. Your code is strong -- the migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team.
lines.speak: No. Your code is strong. The migration script was the best work on the team. But when a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team.

lines.html: Nobody told me it was a problem.
lines.speak: Nobody told me it was a problem.

lines.html: That is on me, and I am sorry. You should be aware of it. So -- what is getting in the way?
lines.speak: That is on me, and I am sorry. You should be aware of it. So, what is getting in the way?

lines.html: The reviews. I wait three days for a code review, every sprint.
lines.speak: The reviews. I wait three days for a code review, every sprint.

lines.html: That I can fix. I need you to flag it on day three, and I will adjust the review queue. Deal?
lines.speak: That I can fix. I need you to flag it on day three, and I will adjust the review queue. Deal?

comp: How does Marina react at the start?
She gets defensive: she says the tickets came late, and it is not her fault.
What is the real cause of the delay?
She waits three days for a code review, every sprint.
For never telling her that it was a problem.

quickfire.situation: Open the conversation. She does not know why she is here.
tips: Say it is not easy. Do not smile it away.

quickfire.situation: Describe the behavior in one sentence, with a number.
tips: A date, a number, one event.

quickfire.situation: She says: 'It is not my fault.' Answer.
tips: Return to the fact, and to the impact.

quickfire.situation: Name the impact on the team, not on you.
tips: Who pays for it? Say their name.

quickfire.situation: Ask the question -- and then be quiet.
Close with one clear ask, and one true piece of praise.

items: Are you saying I am a bad developer?
Why are you telling me this now, and not three sprints ago?
Does Sophia know about this conversation?
pressure.followup: Answer first, fact second, ask third. Never negotiate the fact -- and never repeat the label she puts on herself.

heading: Say It to
Marina
guided.sub: Use the feedback note. Four moves, in this order:
chips: can I give you some feedback?
the last three sprints closed two days late
what is getting in the way?

heading: She Does Not
Accept It
semi.quote: That is not fair. Everybody is late on this project.
semi.sub: She defends herself twice: first she blames the tickets, then she blames the whole team. Do not argue, do not get harsh. Return to the fact, name the impact, and ask your question until she answers it.
chips: what is getting in the way?

heading: Your Real
Conversation
free.headline: Two minutes. No notes. One behavior.
free.sub: Take someone at Oracle you should have talked to, and never did. Give the feedback out loud: the behavior with a number, the impact, the question, the ask. End with something true that you praise.

survival.en: Can I give you some feedback?
The last three sprints closed two days late.
survival.pt: As três últimas sprints fecharam dois dias atrasadas.

survival.en: When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days.
survival.pt: Quando uma sprint atrasa, o impacto são dois dias perdidos.

survival.en: What is getting in the way?
I need you to flag the risk on day three.
survival.pt: Preciso que você sinalize o risco no terceiro dia.

checks: I can open a hard conversation: "Can I give you some feedback?"
I describe behavior with a number, never a label.
I can name the impact on the team.
I ask "What is getting in the way?" -- and then I am quiet.
I close with one clear ask, and praise only what is true.

closing.line: You said the difficult thing, she defended herself, and you still found the real problem.
closing.next_desc: Raising a risk out loud, before it becomes a disaster

order_audio.text: Diogo opens the conversation and asks if he can give feedback. He describes the behavior: the last three sprints closed two days late. Marina gets defensive, and says the tickets came late. Diogo names the impact on the test team, and asks what is getting in the way. Marina says she waits three days for a code review, and Diogo asks her to flag it on day three.
items: He describes the behavior: three sprints closed two days late.
He names the impact and asks what is getting in the way.
Diogo asks if he can give her some feedback.
Marina says the code reviews take three days, and he asks her to flag it earlier.
Marina gets defensive, and says the tickets came late.

preclass.desc: Say the difficult thing without losing the person: behavior, impact, question, ask.
preclass.context: Marina is a strong developer, and her last three sprints closed late. Diogo does not give a speech. He is specific: "The last three sprints closed two days late." That is a fact about her behavior, not a label about her -- and a fact can be discussed. She gets defensive anyway: "That is not my fault." He does not get harsh. He goes back to the impact: "When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days for the test team." Maybe she was not aware of that. Then he asks the question that changes everything -- "What is getting in the way?" -- and he is quiet. The answer is the code review queue, and he can adjust it. He ends with real praise: her migration script was the best work on the team. The feedback took fifteen minutes, and she did most of the talking.
quiz_ctx: Why does Diogo say "the last three sprints closed two days late"?
Because it is specific behavior, not a label about her.
Because he wants her to feel guilty.
Because Sophia asked him to say it.
I need you to flag the risk on day three.
I need you flag the risk on day three.
I need that you flag the risk on day three.
He is quiet, and she gives the real reason.
He explains the plan for ten minutes.
preclass.tip_sub: Falar o difícil sem atacar a pessoa: fato, impacto, pergunta, pedido. / Saying the hard thing without attacking the person.
tip_rows: Past simple + número (nunca adjetivo sobre a pessoa)
The last three sprints closed two days late.
When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days.
What is getting in the way? + I need you to + verbo
What is getting in the way? ... I need you to flag it earlier.
preclass.tip_key: Depois de want / need / ask, o inglês usa you TO + verbo ("I need you to flag") -- nunca "I need you flag" nem "I need that you flag". E a regra de ouro: fato, nunca rótulo. "You are careless" só tem uma resposta possível: defesa.

blanks.hint: Hint: what you tell someone about their work / devolutiva
blanks.phrase: Can I give you some feedback?

blanks.hint: Hint: what a person does, not who they are / comportamento
blanks.phrase: I am talking about the behavior, not about you.

blanks.hint: Hint: what it costs the team / impacto
blanks.phrase: When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days.

blanks.hint: Hint: the question that opens the real problem / o que atrapalha
blanks.phrase: What is getting in the way?

blanks.hint: Hint: need + you + ___ + verb / partícula antes do verbo
blanks.phrase: I need you to flag the risk on day three.
blanks.post: flag the risk on day three."

blanks.hint: Hint: too hard, and cruel / duro demais
blanks.phrase: Honest is good. Harsh is not.

preclass.speech_intro: The five lines of hard feedback. Record each one. Calm and slow -- if your voice is hard, she stops listening.
quiz_sit: Someone on your team is late for the third sprint. You open with:
"Can I give you some feedback? The last three sprints closed two days late."
"You are always late. Everybody has noticed."
An email to the whole team about deadlines.
She says: "That is not my fault." You answer:
"I am not talking about fault. When a sprint closes late, the impact is two lost days."
"Yes, it is. You are the only one who is late."
"Fine, forget it. It is not important."
After the feedback, the best next move is:
Ask "What is getting in the way?" and stay quiet.
Explain your plan for ten minutes.
Give three more examples from last year.
think: Take someone at Oracle you should have given feedback to, and never did. Speak for two minutes: (1) open the conversation, (2) the behavior, with a number, (3) the impact on the team, (4) the question, (5) one clear ask. Record it.
Now she answers: "Everybody on this project is late. Why me?" Answer out loud. Do not argue, do not get harsh -- go back to the fact, name the impact, and ask your question again. Record it.

complementary.title: Ted Lasso (2020-2023)
complementary.desc: Ted often tells players hard truths about their work. He is direct, but he stays kind and gives a clear next step.
complementary.tip: Pick one coaching scene and note how he starts the difficult part of the conversation.

complementary.title: Skills 360 -- Giving and Receiving Feedback (1)
complementary.desc: Feedback is not only for your boss to give. This lesson shows how to give feedback to anyone at work.
complementary.tip: Take notes on the structure, then plan one real feedback conversation using it.

complementary.title: How to Give Feedback at Work -- Professional English
complementary.desc: A business English lesson with sentences for giving hard feedback in a professional way.
complementary.tip: Write 3 sentences about a real problem with a colleague, using the phrases from the video.

teacher.title: Abertura (2 min): Compartilhe a tela. Diga: 'Last class you disagreed with power. Today the power is yours -- and it is worse. You have to tell someone on your team that she is failing.' Avise: hoje ele fala quase o tempo todo.
teacher.warmup: Warm-up falado (4 min): Toque a frase da Aula 11. Depois a virada: 'Now nobody can overrule you. That is exactly why it is hard.' Faca a pergunta do box e cale a boca por 90 segundos.
teacher.goal: Objetivo (1 min): 'Words, a short text, and then you say the difficult thing -- three times, and I get defensive.' Rapido.
teacher.vocab_transition: Transicao vocab (1 min): 'Eight words. Click each card.' Ferramenta, nao aula de vocabulario.
teacher.vocab1: Vocab reveal 1-4 (4 min): Pista, tentativa, reveal, audio, uma frase real. CCQ 'behavior': 'Is "lazy" a behavior? (No -- it is a label. A behavior is what the person DID.)' CCQ 'specific': 'Is "often late" specific? (No. "Three sprints, two days each" is.)'
teacher.vocab2: Vocab reveal 5-8 (4 min): Mesma dinamica. CCQ 'defensive': 'When someone is defensive, are they listening? (No.)' CCQ 'harsh': 'Is honest the same as harsh? (No.)' CCQ 'praise': 'Should you praise something that is not true? (Never.)'
teacher.vocab_check: Vocab check falado (4 min): 90 segundos sobre alguem com quem ele deveria ter falado e nunca falou. Minimo cinco palavras novas. Voce so escuta. Se travar, aponte um chip -- nunca traduza.
teacher.ch3: Transicao leitura (1 min): 'Short text. Read it fast -- then tell me if it works with a senior developer who is older than you.' A leitura e trampolim.
teacher.reading: Leitura + gist (5 min): 90 segundos de leitura silenciosa. Depois o gist. E entao a pergunta que vale a aula: 'In Brazil, at Oracle, would you really say this to her face?' Dois minutos de fala dele. Discorde de proposito.
teacher.tf: True / False (3 min): Ele decide em voz alta ANTES de clicar, e justifica com uma frase do texto. Rapido.
teacher.bank: Useful language falado (4 min): Cada frase lida em voz alta e imediatamente reescrita para uma pessoa real do time dele. Seis frases da tela, seis frases dele. E o esqueleto dos role-plays.
teacher.grammar: Grammar discovery (4 min): Toque as 4 frases. Pergunta unica: 'Which one attacks? Which one describes? Which one opens a conversation?' Guie: past simple + numero = fato; when + impact = consequencia; what is getting in the way = abre.
teacher.rule: Reveal the Rule (3 min): Clique. Fato, impacto, pergunta. Exija 2 frases dele com 'When... the impact is...' sobre o time real. CCQ: 'Is "you are careless" a fact? (No -- it is a label.)'
teacher.mistake: Common Mistake (2 min): Rotulo vs fato, e o 'want you TO'. Peca as versoes certas em voz alta, duas vezes. CCQ: 'I want you send it -- correct? (No: I want you TO send it.)'
teacher.ch5: Transicao (1 min): 'She is good. And she is late. Both are true.' Suba a tensao.
teacher.listening1: Listening 1 (4 min): 'Sophia will not have this conversation for you.' Toque uma vez, sem texto. Perguntas no proximo slide.
teacher.listening1_comp: Comprehension falada (3 min): Respostas em voz alta antes do clique. Depois: 'Why does she say -- do not send an email?' Deixe ele deduzir falando.
teacher.artefact: Artefato (3 min): Nao leia por ele. 'Read it, then give me the feedback out loud, in four moves.' Ele fala; voce marca o que faltou. Este card e o roteiro do role-play guiado.
teacher.dialogue: Dialogo (6 min): Voce e a Marina, e voce fica na DEFENSIVA de verdade: culpa os tickets, depois o time todo. Antes de cada fala do Diogo, ELE fala primeiro; so entao clique e toque o audio. Repare: ele nunca discute culpa.
teacher.dialogue_comp: Dialogue comprehension (2 min): Perguntas sobre a MARINA. Depois: 'Whose fault was it, in the end?' Deixe ele defender a resposta -- a resposta e: do processo, e do proprio Diogo, que nao falou antes.
teacher.listening2: Listening 2 (4 min): 'This is the feedback, done well -- 60 seconds.' Toque uma vez. Depois: 'What is the behavior? What is the impact? What is the ask?' -- ele fala.
teacher.ch6: Transicao (1 min): 'From here you give the feedback, and I defend myself. Three times.' Daqui ate o fim, ele fala quase sozinho.
teacher.quickfire: Quick Fire (5 min): Uma situacao por vez, resposta imediata. Sem correcao entre elas -- fluencia primeiro. Tips so em ultimo caso.
teacher.rp_guided: Role-play GUIADO (4 min): Voce e a Marina. Ele da o feedback usando os quatro movimentos do artefato. Nao interrompa; no fim marque o que faltou. Se ele der um discurso e nao fizer a pergunta, mande refazer.
teacher.rp_semi: Role-play SEMI-LIVRE (5 min): Agora voce se defende duas vezes: 'the tickets came late' e depois 'everybody is late on this project'. Ele nao pode discutir culpa nem endurecer o tom. Tem de voltar ao fato, nomear o impacto e repetir a pergunta ate voce responder.
teacher.rp_free: Role-play LIVRE (5 min): Sem apoio. 'A real person at Oracle. Two minutes. One behavior, one ask. Go.' NAO interrompa, cronometre e diga o tempo no fim. So entao 2 correcoes.
teacher.pressure: Under pressure (4 min): Tres perguntas duras, uma a uma, sem aviso. Resposta primeiro, fato depois. Se hesitar mais de 3 segundos, repita a pergunta.
teacher.survival: Survival Card (3 min): 'Five phrases. With these you can start any hard conversation.' Toque cada uma; ele repete em tom calmo e devagar -- tom duro mata o conteudo.
teacher.learned: Wrap-up (3 min): Ele clica cada check e diz a frase que prova. So depois devolva as 2 correcoes. Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos dando um feedback real (comportamento + impacto + pergunta + pedido).
teacher.closing: Fechamento (1 min): Homework ORAL: gravar 2 minutos de feedback real e enviar. Anuncie a Aula 13: 'Next time you raise a risk -- before it explodes, and in front of people who do not want to hear it.'
